skill.name: developing-genkit-js
skill.description: Guides development of Genkit applications in Node.js and TypeScript, including setup, flows, tools, model selection, CLI usage, and troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Build and troubleshoot Genkit JavaScript and TypeScript AI workflows.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but semantic review shows most are Markdown examples or benign Genkit documentation. Confirmed risk is limited to expected developer workflows that may run CLI/package-manager commands and modify project files, so publication is acceptable with a medium warning.

medium_findings.title: External Development Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to run Genkit, npm, npx, and watcher commands. This is expected for a Genkit development skill, but these commands can execute local or package-sourced code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and relevant to the stated Genkit workflow. I found no evidence that they are used for stealth, persistence, or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Workspace File Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The setup guide asks the assistant to create directories, create Genkit initialization files, and edit package.json scripts. This is legitimate setup behavior but can change user projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file creation and package script edits are clearly documented. The risk is bounded because the instructions describe normal project setup and do not hide the changes.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced TypeScript or shell examples. They are instructions and examples, not embedded executable code in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows Markdown and TypeScript documentation. No runnable script file, hook, or hidden execution path was found in these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits occur in words such as description, describe, design, or Genkit documentation text. I found no cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose or schema descriptions, not hashing or encryption calls. No evidence found for DES, MD5, SHA-1, or similar weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Network and Secret Mentions Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The network and environment findings refer to localhost UI documentation, an example.com sample image URL, and guidance to store API keys in environment variables. No credential exfiltration pattern was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are transparent setup guidance and placeholder examples. I found no code that reads secrets and sends them to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential-access heuristic is explained by developer documentation. No obfuscation, hidden network call, prompt injection, or data exfiltration intent was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are visible documentation for package commands, environment variables, and sample media. The semantic review did not find malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Development Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs assistants to run Genkit, npm, npx, and watcher commands. This is expected for a Genkit development skill, but these commands can execute local or package-sourced code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and relevant to the stated Genkit workflow. I found no evidence that they are used for stealth, persistence, or exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace File Modification Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide asks the assistant to create directories, create Genkit initialization files, and edit package.json scripts. This is legitimate setup behavior but can change user projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file creation and package script edits are clearly documented. The risk is bounded because the instructions describe normal project setup and do not hide the changes.

content.value_statement: Genkit API changes make JavaScript AI apps hard to implement correctly. This skill guides setup, flows, tools, models, CLI usage, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Explains current Genkit JavaScript and TypeScript imports, flow definitions, and model references.
Guides new project setup with provider plugins, an ai directory, flow folders, and a Genkit UI script.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for common Genkit v1.x migration and type-check errors.
Shows examples for text generation, structured output, streaming, multimodal image generation, and speech output.
Directs assistants to use Genkit CLI documentation search and read commands for current API details.
limitations: It does not include runnable source files or automated tests.
It depends on the local Genkit CLI and project package manager being available.
Provider-specific setup still requires valid user credentials and current provider documentation.
Some model recommendations may change and should be verified before production use.

use_cases.title: Start a Genkit Project
use_cases.description: Create a clean Genkit setup with provider plugins, shared initialization, and starter directories for flows and tools.

use_cases.title: Migrate Genkit Code
use_cases.description: Replace deprecated pre-1.0 imports, response access patterns, model identifiers, and flow definitions with current Genkit syntax.

use_cases.title: Debug AI Workflow Errors
use_cases.target_user: Developer fixing build or runtime errors
use_cases.description: Map validation, type, streaming, multimodal, and provider errors to documented fixes before trying unrelated changes.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Genkit
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Genkit in this TypeScript project. Check whether Genkit already exists, choose a provider with me, and add only the starter files needed.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Genkit flow for this feature. Use current Genkit v1.x imports, clear input and output schemas, and a provider model that matches the project.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Genkit Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Debug this Genkit error. First compare it against the common errors reference, then explain the likely cause and make the smallest correct fix.

prompt_templates.title: Design Advanced Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Genkit workflow for streaming or multimodal generation. Verify current CLI documentation, include schemas, and call out provider-specific requirements.

output_examples.input: Set up Genkit in a new Next.js app with Google AI.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks existing dependencies, adds Genkit initialization, creates starter AI folders, and adds a Genkit UI script without running it.

output_examples.input: Fix an error caused by response.text() in existing Genkit code.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the deprecated response access pattern and changes it to direct property access after checking the common errors reference.

output_examples.input: Add an image generation flow with Gemini.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses the image model, includes text and image response modalities, and warns that provider credentials must be configured separately.

best_practices: Verify current Genkit documentation with the CLI before changing APIs or model names.
Keep flows, tools, and shared Genkit initialization in separate modules with clear exports.
Store provider credentials in environment variables and avoid hardcoding secrets in source files.
anti_patterns: Do not use pre-1.0 Genkit imports from internal packages.
Do not run package installation, Genkit UI, or watcher commands without user awareness.
Do not assume audio, image, or streaming response formats without checking provider requirements.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Genkit AI applications in Node.js and TypeScript, including flows, tools, provider setup, and examples.

faq.question: Does it support current Genkit APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. The references focus on Genkit v1.x patterns and warn against older imports and response methods.

faq.question: Can it install dependencies automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide dependency checks and installation commands, but package changes should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Which provider does it use by default?
faq.answer: It defaults to Google AI when the user does not choose a provider, while allowing other providers through documentation lookup.

faq.question: Does it handle multimodal generation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples and warnings for image generation, speech generation, and required response modalities.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium warning because it instructs filesystem edits, package scripts, and CLI commands for development workflows.
